Metallic uranium exists in three allootropic forms: the orthorhombic alpha form, stable below $663^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; the tetragonal beta form which exists between $663^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $770^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; and the body-centered cubic form which exists at higher temperatures ( $>770^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

A potential of +1.80 volts for the half-cell reaction, $\mathrm{U} \rightarrow \mathrm{U}^{+3} + 3\mathrm{e}$ , places it below beryllium and above hafnium and aluminum in the electromotive force series.

Uranium combines with most elements to form a large number and variety of compounds. "Gmelins Handbuch der Anorganischen Chemie," $^{6}$ which surveys the literature through the year 1935, describes several hundred compounds.

Katz and Seaborg $^{2}$ describes some of the more recently prepared compounds, principally of organic character, such as chelates, alkoxides, amides, mercaptides, and $\pi$ -cyclopentadienyl compounds.

Complex salts such as $3(\mathrm{CN}_3\mathrm{H}_6)_2\mathrm{CO}_3\cdot$ $\mathrm{U(CO_3)_2\cdot 4H_2O}$ and $2(\mathrm{NH_4})_2\mathrm{C_2O_4}\cdot \mathrm{U(C_2O_4)_2}\cdot 6\mathrm{H_2O}$ form an important group of uranium (IV) compounds.

Inorganic compounds of pentavalent uranium are $\mathrm{UF}_5$ , $\mathrm{UCl}_5$ , $\mathrm{UCl}_5\cdot \mathrm{SOCl}_2$ , $\underline{\underline{\mathbf{8}}}$ , $\mathrm{UCl}_5\cdot \mathrm{PCl}_5$ , $\underline{\underline{\mathbf{6}}}$ and $\mathrm{UF}_5\cdot \mathrm{xHF}$ . $\underline{\underline{\mathbf{6}}}$ $\mathrm{UOCl}_3$ has been reported as an intermediate compound in the chlorination of uranium oxides with carbon tetrachloride.
Hexavalent uranium is represented by $\mathrm{UF}_6$ , $\mathrm{UCl}_6$ , $\mathrm{UO}_3$ , uranates, and uranyl $(\mathrm{UO}_2^{2+})$ compounds.

Tripositive uranium, $U^{+3}$ .
Evidence for the existence of $U^{+3}$ comes from the reversibility of the $U(III)/U(IV)$ couple.

Chloride, bromide, iodide, perchlorate and sulfate solutions of uranium(III) have been reported. $^{11}$ They are deep red in color and unstable, with oxidation of $U^{+3}$ to $U^{+4}$ occurring and hydrogen being evolved.
Strongly acidified solutions $^{12}$ or those kept at low temperatures $^{10}$ appear to be more stable.
Tetrapositive uranium, $\mathbf{U}^{+4}$ .

The existence of the $\mathbf{U}^{+4}$ ion in solution has been confirmed by measurement of the acid liberated on dissolving $\mathrm{UCl}_{4}\frac{13}{4}$ and by solvent extraction studies of U(IV) with thenoyltrifluoroacetone $^{14a}$ and acetylacetone. $^{14b}$ Uranous solutions may be prepared by dissolution of a water-soluble salt: the chloride, bromide, iodide, or sulfate; by dissolution of uranium or a uranium compound in an appropriate solvent, e.g., uranium metal in sulfuric or phosphoric acid; or by reduction of a uranyl solution by chemical, electrochemical or photochemical means.

Uranium(IV) undergoes hydrolysis with evidence in the first stages for the formation of the mononuclear species, $\mathrm{UOH}^{+3}$ . $13,15-18$ Polymeric species also are formed which apparently are not in equilibrium with the monomer. $^{13,15,19}$ Hietanen $^{17}$ found that in addition to the monomeric species, a polymer of the type $\mathrm{U[(OH)_3U]_n^{4+n}}$ could account for the hydrolysis of uranium(IV) to good approximation.

Pentapositive uranium, UO $\frac{3}{2}$ .

The existence of uranium(V) ion in solution has been confirmed by polarographic measurements. $\frac{24 - 26}{5}$ Support for the $\mathrm{UO}_2^+$ ion comes from the reversibility of the U(V)/U(VI) couple and from infrared and crystallographic studies of uranium and transuranic elements.
The $\mathrm{UO}_2^+$ ion is most stable in the pH range of 2 to 4.
Hexapositive uranium, $\mathsf{UO}_{2}^{+2}$ .
A number of physical-chemical measurements as well as crystallographic, infrared and Raman spectra studies support the existence of U(VI) ion as $\mathsf{UO}_{2}^{+2}$ .
uranyl ion may be reduced by reducing agents $^{34}$ or by electrochemical or photochemical means.

Uranyl perchlorate is apparently completely dissociated; $^{10}$ whereas, uranyl fluoride is undissociated and tends to form dimers (see section on complex ion formation - IV-C2). $^{35,36}$ Hydrolysis of the uranyl ion has been the subject of extensive investigation.

Considerable evidence has been adduced for the formation of polymeric species of the type $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{UO}_3)_n^{2+}$ . $^{37-47}$ According to Sutton, $^{40}$ formation of polymers beyond the trimer $\mathrm{U}_3\mathrm{O}_8^{2+}$ is negligible.

However, the trimer itself may undergo further hydrolysis with the formation of $\mathrm{U}_3\mathrm{O}_8(\mathrm{OH})^+$ , $\mathrm{U}_3\mathrm{O}_8(\mathrm{OH})_2^-$ , and eventually anionic species. $^{40,41}$ Ahrland, $^{42}$ in his original paper, proposed the formation of the monomer $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{OH})^+$ as well as polynuclear species.
In a reappraisal of the work, Arhland, Hietanen, and Sillén $^{43}$ stated that there was no certain indication of mononuclear complexes being formed.
Rather, the experimental data was explained on the basis that complex ions of the type $\mathrm{UO}_2[(\mathrm{OH})_2\mathrm{UO}_2]_n^{2+}$ were formed.
From the data it was not possible to distinguish between a limited mechanism in which n varied from 1 to 3 or 4 or an unlimited mechanism in which n assumed all integral values.
Kraus $^{19}$ suggested that reactions leading to the formation of polymers may have a less positive value of $\Delta H$ than the reaction leading to the formation of the monomer.

This is apparently the case as was shown by Hearne and White $^{46}$ who determined the enthalpy change to be 20.8 kcal/mole for the monomeric reaction $(\mathrm{UO}_2\mathrm{OH}^+$ formed) and 6.7 kcal/mole for the dimeric reaction $(\mathrm{U}_2\mathrm{O}_5^{2+}$ formed).
Included in the table are values of the equilibrium constant $\mathbf{K}_{1}$ , the constant for the formation of the monomeric species.
This constant has been evaluated by at least seven groups of investigators $^{25c,38,42,48-51}$ exclusive of Hearne and White $^{46}$ .
The values obtained agree very well ( $\log^{*} \mathbf{K}_{1} = -4.09$ to $-4.70$ ).

However, the experimental conditions $^{25c,38,50}$ and assumptions $^{25c,38}$ used in some of the evaluations have been questioned. $^{46}$ Also, the re-evaluation of Ahrland's $^{42}$ work already has been mentioned, and Rydberg $^{51}$ has proposed an explanation for not detecting polynuclear species in his experiments.

None-the-less, one must concur with Rydberg $^{51}$ who wrote, "...it seems remarkable that the same constants should be obtained for a fictive mononuclear hydrolysis product with different U(VI) concentrations and so different methods of investigation---."
Numerous complexes have been reported. $^{34}$ However, the amount of quantitative data for the various ligands is rather limited and often contradictory.

Inorganic complexes of uranium(IV) which have been recognized through the formation of complex salts include the fluoride, chloride, sulfate, sulfite, and phosphate. $^{34}$ Table IV lists the equilibrium constants and thermodynamic data available for some of the uranium(IV) complexes in aqueous solution.
In addition, a carbonate complex, possibly $\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{CO}_3)_5^{6-}$ , has been found to be stable in solutions of excess carbonate or bicarbonate ions. $^{63}$
Tishkoff has calculated dissociation constants for acetate complexes on the basis of the oxygenated uranium(IV) ion $\mathrm{UO}^{2+}$ being formed.

Inorganic uranium(VI) complexes which have been identified through the formation of crystalline salts include the fluorides, chlorides, nitrates, sulfates, carbonates, cyanides, and phosphates. $^{34}$ Uranyl solutions with these anions present have been studied.

For example, evidence for some complexing of the uranyl ion with nitrate $^{74,76,77}$ and chloride $^{67,76,77,105-107}$ ions has been reported by some investigators; but a complete lack of evidence has been reported by others. $^{50}$ Day and Powers $^{77}$ have pointed out that the constants calculated by them are concentration constants rather than activity constants.
Other investigators, however, who have corrected their results to apply to pure aqueous solutions have found some complexing to occur with the chloride ion. $^{67,105-107}$

Ahrland and co-workers have determined equilibrium constants for the formation of complexes $\mathrm{UO_2F^+}$ , $\mathrm{UO_2F_2}$ , $\mathrm{UO_2F_3^-}$ , and $\mathrm{UO_2F_4^{2-}}$ and found no evidence for the dimerization of $\mathrm{UO_2F_2}$ for uranyl ion concentrations less than 0.1M.

The oxalate ion, $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}$ , has been proposed by some investigators as the complexing ligand; the bioxalate ion, $\mathrm{HC}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{-}$ , by others and Heidt has

In more recent work, Moskvin and Zakharova conclude that complexes may be formed with both $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{HC}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{-}$ ions and that the amount of each formed will depend upon its stability and the conditions of the experiment.

For example, Hox-Bernstrom,138 in her work with salicylic acid, $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{A}$ , and methoxybenzoic acid, HB, was able to show that complexes of the type $\mathrm{UO_2(H_2A)(H^+)}_{-1}$ , $\mathrm{UO_2(H_2A)(H^+)}_{-2}$ , and $\mathrm{UO_2(HB)(H^+)}_{-2}$ were formed in the aqueous phase.

For salicylic acid, the complexes $\mathrm{UO_2(HA)^+}$ corresponding to $\mathrm{UO_2(H_2A)(H^+)}_{-1}$ and $\mathrm{UO_2(HA)(OH)}$ or $\mathrm{UO_2A}$ corresponding to $\mathrm{UO_2(H_2A)(H^+)}_{-2}$ were postulated.

Some of the complexing agents studied recently include dihydroxy-maleic acid, $\frac{148}{2}$ triose-reductone $\frac{149,150}{2}$ (enoltartronaldehyde), reductic acid $\frac{151}{2}$ (cyclopentene-2-diol-2,3-one-1), complexes $\frac{152,153}{2}$ (iminodiacetic acid and its derivatives), xanthates and dithiocarbamates, $\frac{154}{2}$ protoporphyrin, $\frac{155}{2}$ o-cresotic acid, $\frac{156}{2}$ miricitrine, $\frac{157}{2}$ dialkylphosphoric acids, $\frac{158,159}{2}$ and pyrazolone derivatives.
(1) In a given homologous series, the solubility decreases as the molecular weight of the solvent increases.
At $10^{-5}\underline{\underline{M}}$ the salt is approximately 40 per cent dissociated, $169$ i.e., $\Lambda \eta /120 = 0.4$ .
Uranyl perchlorate at this concentration is almost completely ionized. $169$ Ion association occurs at higher concentrations, but significantly less than for uranyl nitrate.
At approximately 0.01M, the association of uranyl perchlorate is maximum ( $\Lambda \eta /120$ has a minimum value of $-0.1$ ) in the concentration range $10^{-5}$ to $1\underline{\underline{M}}$ .
The ionization of this salt may well be associated with the amount of water contained within the tributyl phosphate since the electrical conductivity is decreased by dehydration. $169$

types the absorption spectra of uranyl nitrate in a number of solvents and solvent mixtures.\* Differences between types were attributed to a series of hydrated and solvated nitrate complexes, $\mathrm{UO}_2^{2 + }$ $\mathrm{UO}_2\mathrm{NO}_3^+$ $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{NO}_3)_2$ , and $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{NO}_3)_3^-$ .

It is interesting to note that the absorption spectrum of uranyl nitrate in tributyl phosphate (0.016 - 1.6 M) is characteristic of the complex $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{NO}_3)_2$ and indicates little ionization. $^{168}$ A similar spectrum is given by uranyl nitrate in methyl isobutyl ketone (0.02 M).78 Uranyl perchlorate in methyl isobutyl ketone (0.02 M), however, exhibits a spectrum characteristic of the uranyl ion, $\mathrm{UO}_2^{2 + }$ .
These results appear to be in general agreement with those obtained through conductivity-viscosity measurements. $^{167-169}$

The trinitratouranyl complex $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{NO}_3)_3$ has been studied by a number of workers.78,79,171-173 It is formed by the addition of a second soluble nitrate to a solution of uranyl nitrate in a non-aqueous solvent such as anhydrous nitric acid,171 dinitrogen tetroxide,172 acetone,78,79 methyl isobutyl ketone,78 dibutyl ether,173 etc.
Kaplan and coworkers also report that the complex is formed in 16 M nitric acid, but that its formation is far from complete.

The negative character of the complex has been demonstrated by electrolytic transference experiments.78 Its composition has been deduced by the isolation of solid compounds from solutions of the type described above79,171,172 and by the similarity of the absorption spectrum of such solutions with that of crystalline caesium uranyl nitrate, $\mathrm{CaUO}_2(\mathrm{NO}_3)_3^{78,79}$

The formation of chlorouranyl complexes in non-aqueous solvents has been reported by Vdovenko, Lipovskii, and Nikitinas $^{108}$ The complexes $\mathrm{UO}_2\mathrm{Cl}^+$ , $\mathrm{UO}_2\mathrm{Cl}_2$ , $\mathrm{UO}_2\mathrm{Cl}_3^-$ were formed by the addition of pyridine hydrochloride or hydroxylamine hydrochloride to a solution of uranyl perchlorate or uranyl chloride in acetone.

In partition studies of uranyl nitrate between aqueous solution and organic solvent (alcohols, esters, ethers and ketones) it is generally found that the water content of the organic phase increases with uranyl nitrate concentration. $^{163,167*}$ For alcohols, the relation between the water content $M_w$ and uranyl nitrate concentration $M_u$ appears to be a complex function. $^{**}$ For esters, ethers, and ketones the relation is linear except possibly at high values of $M_u$ .
For many of the linear solvents h is very nearly 4.0.163,167 This has been interpreted by Katzin to mean that the species $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O})_4(\mathrm{NO}_3)_2$ is extracted.
The latter view is supported by isopiestic measurements.176

The isolation of solvated uranium salts, in particular uranyl nitrate, is reported in the literature.[6, 163,180-182] In phase studies of ternary systems: uranyl nitrate, water, organic solvent, Katzin and Sullivan[163] have concluded that uranyl nitrate in aqueous solution is largely hexasolvated, subject to the activity.
As organic molecules are dissolved, 2,3,4 and perhaps 6 water molecules may be displaced, depending upon the electron-donor capabilities of the organic molecules.
The particular stability of the final two water molecules is in agreement with the results of Ryaskin and co-workers $^{177,178}$ obtained through infrared absorption measurements.

The ability of solvents to displace water is in the order: alcohols > ethers > ketones. $^{163}$ This general order of solvate stability is confirmed by heat of solution measurements. $^{183}$ It is in agreement also with the order of base (electron-donor) strengths of the solvents determined by other means. $^{163,183}$ Methyl isobutyl ketone is anomalous in that it behaves stronger toward uranyl nitrate than its base strength would indicate. $^{163,183}$ Tributyl phosphate, according to heat measurements, competes with water almost as well as diethyl ether and isobutyl alcohol. $^{183}$
This suggests a competition between solvent molecule and nitrate ion for coordination with the uranyl ion. $^{171}$
A saturated solution of uranyl nitrate hexahydrate in tributyl phosphate corresponds closely to the unhydrated disolvated compound $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{NO}_3)_2\cdot 2\mathrm{TBP}$ .
Evidence for the existence of the single species is:168,187
(1) The solubility is not appreciably temperature dependent over the range $0 - 50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
(2) On freezing and rewarming a saturated solution, a sharp melting-point of $-6.0 \pm 0.5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is observed.
(3) The mole ratio of uranyl nitrate to TBP approaches the value 1:2 asymptotically under a variety of conditions.

(4) The effect of inert diluents for the TBP on uranyl nitrate partition coefficients supports a 1:2 formula, i.e., the partition coefficient of uranyl nitrate varies as the square of the TBP concentration.
Jezowska - Trzebiatowska, et al<sup>80</sup> report that the absorption spectrum of uranyl nitrate in tributyl phosphate gives no indication of the formation of a stable complex.
Attempts to identify a complex species in the concentration range 0.02-0.06M were unsuccessful.
Hesford and McKay<sup>169</sup> report evidence for the formation of $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{Cl}_4)_2\cdot 2\mathrm{TBP}$ under certain conditions.
From a 10.3M aqueous perchloric acid solution, the partition coefficient of uranium varies as the square of the TBP concentration in benzene.
Jezowska - Trzebiatowska, et al<sup>80</sup> report the formation of a 1:1 complex between $\mathrm{UCl}_4$ and TBP in methyl alcohol.

Tributyl phosphine oxide, like tributyl phosphate, forms an anhydrous disolvate with uranyl nitrate. $^{187}$ Healy and Kennedy $^{188}$ report a number of other neutral organophosphorus solvents which form solvates with uranyl nitrate.
However, not all solutions of the solvent in benzene and saturated in uranyl nitrate give mole ratios of solvent to uranium of 2:1.
For the two diphosphanates and one pyrophosphate studied the mole ratios were 1:1.
The mole ratio in triphenyl phosphate was $\sim 22:1$ .
This is probably the result of the solvent being unable to displace water from the coordination sphere of the uranyl ion. $^{188}$

in conjunction with isopiestic and viscosity measurements, Baes, Zingaro and Coleman $^{158}$ have hypothesized that uranium (VI) is extracted from aqueous perchlorate solutions into n-hexane solutions of d1-(2-ethylhexyl)-phosphate, HL, as the species $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{HL})_2\mathrm{L}_2$ .

As the uranium concentration of the organic phase is increased, there is strong evidence that polymerization occurs. $^{158}$ Similar conclusions have been made by Dyrssen $^{159}$ on the basis of the distribution of uranium (VI) between aqueous perchlorate solution and dibutyl phosphate, HK, in chloroform.

In hexone, the species $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{HK})_2\mathrm{K}_2$ and $\mathrm{UO}_2\mathrm{K}_2$ have been identified. $^{159}$ The extraction of uranium (VI) by dibutyl phosphate from aqueous nitrate solutions into benzene has been studied by Healy and Kennedy. $^{188}$ In addition to the species $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{HK})_2\mathrm{K}_2$ , the polyuranyl species $(\mathrm{UO}_2\mathrm{K}_2)_x\mathrm{2HK}$ and the nitrated species $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{NO}_3)_2\cdot 2\mathrm{HK}$ have been postulated to explain the shape of the extraction curve as a function of nitric acid concentration.

A number of review articles have been written on the analytical chemistry of uranium. $^{6,34,189-200}$ These, together with many texts on chemical analysis (see, for example, references 201-209), serve well as guides to the separation and purification of the element.

More specialized surveys have been made by Hecht $^{210}$ on the quantitative micro-analysis of uranium-bearing minerals, and by Lawrowski $^{211}$ on separation processes for the recovery of nuclear fuels.

In classical systems of analysis, uranium is a member of the third group of elements.* That is, it is not precipitated by hydrochloric acid or by hydrogen sulfide in an acidic solution, but it is precipitated by ammonium hydroxide or ammonium sulfide (see references 204, 206, 208, 213).
Ware<sup>214</sup> has summarized early work using organic rea
She $^{215}$ and Bailey $^{216}$ have investigated some of the more promising ones.
Rodden and Warf $^{34}$ have discussed the use of many reagents, both inorganic and organic, and have described procedures for the use of many of them.
The latter precipitants, i.e., those for which procedures have been given by Rodden and Warf, $^{34}$ are denoted by a dagger (†) in the following discussion.
6,63,201,204,206,217-224 Precipitation of uranium with ammonium, sodium, or potassium carbonates is not very satisfactory.

The solubilities of the respective ammonium, sodium, and potassium salts in water are $50(15^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $^{224}$ $150(\mathrm{RT})$ , $^{225}$ and $71(18^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ ^{218} grams per liter.

The solubility of the potassium salt in a $50\%$ solution of potassium carbonate is 0.200 grams per liter. $^{221}$ The solubility of the sodium salt is decreased by increasing temperature and by increasing sodium salt concentration. $^{223}$
Težak $^{217}$ has studied the precipitation of uranium by ammonium and sodium carbonate.
From a 0.043N uranyl nitrate solution, precipitation was observed to be maximum in the region of 0.1N precipitant concentration.
A suspension of barium carbonate may be used to precipitate uranium. $34,226$ Ammonium salts interfere.
A suspension of basic zinc carbonate may be similarly used. $34,227$ Iron, aluminum and thorium also precipitate.
Alkali cyanides form a yellow precipitate when added to uranyl solutions. $^{34}$
The separation is poor and there are many interferences. $^{34}$ Separation can be made from beryllium in a weakly acidic sulfate solution. $^{228}$
The precipitate is gelatinous and difficult to filter.34 Separation is made from metals complexed by fluoride ions, eg., tantalum and zirconium.
Uranium may be reduced to the (IV)-state with zinc in a solution made slightly acidic.196

The double fluorides, eg., $\mathrm{NaUF}_5$ , are sparingly soluble even in the presence of strong acids.196 Separation can be made under these conditions from Mo, Ti, Ni, Co, Mn, Cu, Fe (II), and V (III).

Reduction to uranium (IV) may be done in the presence of fluorides with iron (II).197,229 Rongalite $(\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S}_2\mathrm{O}_4 \cdot 2\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{O} \cdot 4\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O})$ also has been used to effect reduction.230

However, experimental evidence indicates that the composition of the precipitate depends upon the conditions which exist during precipitation and upon the subsequent treatment, such as washing, which it receives.[231-237]
Uranium is precipitated when a suspension of mercuric oxide is boiled in an aqueous solution containing ammonium chloride. $^{34}$ Separation is made from alkali metals and alkaline earths.
The reaction occurs in the pH range 0.5-3.5.
The optimum range is 2.0-2.5.
Quantitative separation may be effected by freezing the solution, allowing it to stand, and filtering at $2^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The separation from most elements is good since it is done from an acidic solution.34,239 Plutonium, thorium, hafnium, zirconium, and vanadium also precipitate.

Zirconium, thorium, and, to a smaller extent, titanium and tin precipitate.[196-198] Aluminum interferes by the formation of soluble complexes with uranium and phosphate ions.[59] With sulfate and aluminum present, uranium is precipitated in a narrow pH-range around one.
Chromium in excess of 0.2 gram per 100 milliliters causes incomplete precipitation.[196] Large amounts of fluoride ion prevent precipitation.[196]
Sodium hexametaphosphate $\left[\left(\mathrm{NaPO}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]$ also precipitates uranium (IV) from acid solutions.
A $3\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}310_{4}$ solution of uranium (IV) is heated to $60-70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ .

If more than $2 \mathrm{mg}$ of uranium are to be precipitated, a freshly prepared $2 \%$ hexametaphosphate solution is added until its concentration in the precipitating medium is 0.30-0.35 per cent.

To precipitate smaller amounts of uranium, a 0.5 per cent solution of thorium chloride is added as carrier and the hexametaphosphate added until it is in excess 25 per cent with respect to the thorium, i.e., molar ratio of $\mathrm{Th}: \mathrm{PO}_{3}$

Sodium hypophosphite $(\mathrm{NaH}_2\mathrm{PO}_2)$ and ammonium thiosulfate or sulfurous acid precipitate uranium from a boiling, dilute acid solution.[241] Zirconium and titanium precipitate under similar conditions.
Uranium (IV) sulfate is practically insoluble in 47 per cent perchloric acid.

Hydrogen sulfide bubbled through a nearly neutral solution of uranyl salts containing hexamethylene tetramine precipitates uranium in a readily filterable, crystalline form of "uranium red." Separation is made from alkali metals and alkaline earths.34,197

Ammonium metavanadate precipitates ammonium uranyl vanadate from uranyl solutions buffered with ammonium acetate. $^{34}$ Uranovanadic acids are precipitated at pH 2.2-6.5. $^{242}$ Within these limits, the composition does not depend upon the hydrogen ion concentration.

Compounds corresponding to the formulae $\mathrm{H}[\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{OH})_2\mathrm{VO}_3]\cdot \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}, \mathrm{H}[\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{OH})(\mathrm{VO}_3)_2]\cdot 2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O},$ and $\mathrm{H}[\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{VO}_3)_3]\cdot 4\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ have been identified. $^{242}$ Ammonium salts of these acids have been synthesized in the presence of ammonium chloride. $^{242}$ Ammonium uranyltrimetavanadate is the least soluble.
3-Acetyl-4-hydroxycoumarin (3-acetyl benzotetronic acid).

Below pH 1.5 the reagent precipitates.
The thorium complex is soluble in alcohol, but precipitates from an aqueous solution at pH 2-4.

Carbonate ion prevents quantitative precipitation.[189,245]

Benzenearsonic acid† precipitates uranium (IV) in a weakly acidic solution, pH 1-3.
Thorium, zirconium, hafnium, tin (IV), niobium, and tantalum are quantitatively precipitated. $\frac{34}{2}$
Arsonilic acid (p-aminobenzenearsonic acid) precipitates uranium (VI) in a weakly acidic solution, pH 1-4 or greater.
At pH 2.1 or greater the precipitation is quantitative as evidenced by negative ferrocyanide tests of the filtrate.
Other ions which precipitate from neutral or slightly acidic solutions include $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ , $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ , $\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}$ , and $\mathrm{U}^{4+}$ .

Tests on 50-150 mg/1 uranium (VI) in sulfuric acid solutions in the presence of approximately 100- to 1000-fold excess ferrous, sulfate, aluminum, magnesium, and phosphate ions gave nearly $99\%$ precipitation with the reagent.
Optimum conditions for precipitation are pH 1, $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and 10/1 molar ratio of reagent to uranium. $^{248}$
Dibenzoyl methane forms a yellow precipitate with uranium (VI).216
Dimethylammonium dimethyldithiocarbamate forms a red precipitate with uranium (VI).216
Dipropylamine forms a yellow precipitate with uranium.216

Ethylenediamine and uranyl nitrate form an insoluble double salt, $\mathrm{UO}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}(\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4})_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ , in alcoholic-sulfuric acid solution.
Uranium is precipitated when a uranyl acetate solution is boiled with solid reagent. $^{254}$
A brown precipitate results from the reaction of the potassium salt of guaiacol and uranyl acetate in an aqueous solution.[255]
Uranium is precipitated when the reagent is boiled in a uranyl solution that contains ammonium ion and no excess acid. $^{34}$ Ions that form stable complexes with uranium interfere.
a-Hydroxyacetophenone forms a white precipitate with hexavalent uranium.216

precipitated as $\mathrm{UO}_2(\mathrm{C}_9\mathrm{H}_6\mathrm{NO})_2$ - $\mathrm{C}_9\mathrm{H}_7\mathrm{NO}$ from weakly acidic or basic solutions.34 Quantitative recovery has been reported over the pH range 4.1-13.5.

A large number of other elements are precipitated by oxine including Mg, Al, Cr, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Cd, Mo, Bi, and Th.215,260-263 Uranium can be precipitated in the presence of small amounts of complexing agents: fluoride, hydroxylamine, oxalate,lactate, and tartrate.34 Separation from small amounts of phosphate also can be made at pH 10-12 using an excess of oxine.
Tetravalent uranium and oxine form a brownish-yellow deposit.215

A number of other elements precipitate under these conditions including Fe(II), Co, Ni, Ag, Hg(I), and Pb.264 Separation can be made from Mn(II), Zn, and alkaline earth ions.265 In alkali tartrate solutions, uranium can be separated from cobalt and nickel.266
Iron, cobalt, nickel, zinc, cadmium, mercury, and lead are precipitated by the reagent.[267]
Isonitroso-N-phenyl-3-methylpyrazolone.
Uranyl nitrate or acetate forms a reddish-orange precipitate with a $1\%$ solution of the reagent in a $50\%$ alcoholic solution.
In acetate solutions, Ag, Cd, Ni, Co, Zn, Cu(II), and $\mathrm{UO}_2^{2+}$ ions precipitate.
Mercapto-acetic acid forms a greenish-white precipitate with tetravalent uranium.[215,216]
Morpholine precipitates uranium (IV) and (VI) as well as a number of other metal ions.215 A 1 mg per ml solution of uranyl nitrate shows only a yellow color with the reagent.
No precipitate is formed.215
Many other metallic ions are precipitated by the reagent including Al, Cr, Fe(III), Co, Ni, Cu(II), Cd, La, Ce, Hg(II), Bi, Pb, and Th.271
Oxalic acid† precipitates uranium (IV) from acidic solution.34 Strongly complexing organic compounds and fluoride, sulfate, and large amounts of phosphate ions interfere.
Uranium is precipitated from 2-3N hydrochloric acid media.
Immediate filtration of the precipitate may result in losses up to $1\%$ of the uranium to the filtrate.
Precipitation of uranium can be made in cold 1N nitric acid solutions.230 The uranium content should be less than 70 grams per liter.
Enough oxalic acid is added to give a $10\%$ excess of the amount theoretically required to precipitate $\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{O}_4)_2$ .

Pyrogallol and pyridine combine to form a derivative with hexavalent uranium. $\underline{214}$
Quinaldic acid† forms a yellow, amorphous precipitate with uranyl ion.216 Precipitation is made from a neutral or weakly acidic (pH 2-3) solution in the presence of ammonium chloride.

The reagent precipitates a number of metals including copper, zinc, cadmium273 and uranium (IV).215 Uranyl ion is not precipitated in the presence of alkali tartrate274 or a high concentration of acetate ion.215

Under conditions tested, Al, Cr, Fe, Co, Ni, Zn, Cd, $\mathrm{MoO}_4^{2-}$ , and $\mathrm{UO}_2^{2+}$ ions were not precipitated.
Sodium acetate precipitates sodium uranyl acetate from neutral or weakly acidic solutions of uranyl salts. $^{189}$ The method is not very useful for the precipitation of traces of uranium.

The solubility of sodium uranyl acetate in a solution $5\underline{\mathbf{M}}$ in sodium nitrate, $1\underline{\mathbf{M}}$ in acetic acid, and $0.5\underline{\mathbf{M}}$ in sodium acetate is about $100\mathrm{mg}$ per liter. $^{276}$ Neptunium (VI) and plutonium (VI) also precipitate under these conditions.
The addition of sodium acetate and zinc acetate to a neutral or weakly acidic uranyl salt solution precipitates the triple salt, sodium zinc uranyl acetate. $^{189}$

Strychnine in the presence of fluoride ion precipitates hexavalent uranium as $\mathbb{I}(\mathrm{C}_{21}\mathrm{H}_{22}\mathrm{O}_{2}\mathrm{N}_{2}\mathrm{HF})\cdot 6(\mathrm{UO}_{2}\mathrm{F}_{2})\cdot 2\mathrm{HF}$ .
The solubility of the precipitate, in water at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is $47.5\mathrm{mg / 100}$ ml; in $60\%$ alcoholic solution at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $30\mathrm{mg / 100ml}$ .

Rodden and Warf<sup>34</sup> have described the application of several carriers: ferric, aluminum, and calcium hydroxide.

Magnesium oxide and thorium peroxide have been used.<sup>34</sup> The oxide and salts of antimony,<sup>281,282</sup> calcium fluoride,<sup>34</sup> and the phosphates of zirconium,<sup>197</sup> bismuth,<sup>283</sup> and thorium<sup>197,240</sup> have been used to carry uranium from reduced solutions.

These are carried by lanthanum fluoride, ceric and zirconium iodates, ceric and thorium oxalates, barium sulfate, zirconium phosphate, and bismuth arsenate.<sup>276</sup> Uranium (VI) does not carry with these agents providing the concentration of either carrier or uranium is not too large.
The precipitation of uranium in normally precipitating media is inhibited by the formation of soluble complexes. $^{34}$ Carbonate ion is a very efficient complexing agent of uranyl ion.

The decrease in adsorption is less rapid, however, with increased ammonium sulfate concentration than with sulfuric acid.
